title: Buffers, the common-ion effect, and Henderson–Hasselbalch
definition: A **buffer** holds both halves of a weak-acid equilibrium at once — a weak acid $\mathrm{HA}$ **and** a supply of its conjugate base $\mathrm{A^-}$ (from a salt). The extra $\mathrm{A^-}$ is a **common ion**: by Le Chatelier it drives $\mathrm{HA} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H^+} + \mathrm{A^-}$ back to the left, so the acid barely ionizes and the pH settles near $\mathrm{p}K_a$ instead of the low value the acid alone would give. Solving the mass-action law $K_a = [\mathrm{H^+}][\mathrm{A^-}]/[\mathrm{HA}]$ for $[\mathrm{H^+}]$ and taking $-\log_{10}$ gives **Henderson–Hasselbalch**, $\mathrm{pH} = \mathrm{p}K_a + \log_{10}\dfrac{[\mathrm{A^-}]}{[\mathrm{HA}]}$ — not a new law, just $K_a$ in logarithmic form. When the acid and base concentrations are equal the ratio is 1 and $\mathrm{pH} = \mathrm{p}K_a$. A buffer resists change because adding a little $\mathrm{H^+}$ or $\mathrm{OH^-}$ only shifts the large $[\mathrm{A^-}]/[\mathrm{HA}]$ ratio slightly.
